article.title: The Full Story of Musk v. Altman
article.preview_text:
Week 1 of the trial is over. This is the full story from a birthday party in 2015 to a federal courtroom in 2026 — the case mainstream media covered, but never explained.
 
I. THE BIRTHDAY PARTY
The

kept.text:
TL;DR – The Full Backstory of the Musk vs Altman Trial

At a birthday party in 2015, Larry Page (Google’s founder) told Elon Musk that if super-smart AI one day takes over the world, it’s fine - as long as AI survives. When Elon asked “What about humans?”, Page called him a “speciesist”

That moment made Elon decide to create something to protect humanity from dangerous AI. In December 2015, he founded OpenAI as a nonprofit. He named the company, recruited top talent (like Ilya Sutskever), and gave his own money and provided the best resources on the planet

But it all changed when the team had different plans against Elon... the one that makes them rich

Elon started realizing that Sam Altman and the team were quietly planning to turn OpenAI into a for-profit company behind his back. He confronted them directly and made it very clear:
  •  “Either go do something on your own, or keep OpenAI as a nonprofit. I will no longer fund you until you make a firm commitment.”

The very next day, Sam Altman replied:
   •  “I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!”

But in private, co-founder Greg Brockman wrote in his diary:
   •  “I cannot believe that we committed to non-profit… then it was a lie.”

They had already secretly decided to turn OpenAI into a for-profit company

Sam Altman also personally lobbied Brockman in private, telling him that Musk was “dangerous” and “erratic” and shouldn’t be in charge. Brockman flipped. Then he convinced Sutskever to flip too

They secretly teamed up and pushed Elon out because they wanted to convert to for-profit and didn’t want him running it as he didn't wanted to turn it into for profit company. In February 2018, Musk left the board. The people he trusted had betrayed him behind his back

The Final Betrayal:
They partnered with Microsoft and created a “capped-profit” structure with three big promises to protect the original mission:

•   100x profit cap for investors
•   Special “AGI clause” to stop Microsoft if AI got too dangerous
•   Microsoft as exclusive cloud partner

One by one, they removed all these protections. By October 2025, OpenAI became a full for-profit company worth $852 billion today. The people who took Elon’s nonprofit money are now extremely rich

Every time Elon raised concerns about the mission, they tried to offer him equity or money to stay quiet. He refused every single time

In short:
Elon created OpenAI as a charity to save humanity and gave it everything. Altman and the team took his money, lied to him, secretly teamed up to push him out, and turned it into an $852 billion for-profit company for themselves

In the trial, Elon started with one clear message:
"It is not OK to loot charity"

You can go more in-depth into the full backstory in the article below

This number is an Annualized Revenue Run Rate (ARR), which takes the most recent month’s revenue and multiplies it by 12. 

Anthropic hit $30 billion in annualized revenue on April 7, 2026, officially passing OpenAI’s $25 billion.  That’s the verified number. Even so, the growth is extraordinary.

I will explain to you why they earn this much and how the average man benefits from it & make money, bookmark this.

- How they earn this much 

Claude Code: 
A $2.5B agent that builds and deploys software autonomously, replacing junior dev payrolls.

API Scaling: 
75% of revenue comes from pennies per use at massive firms like Deloitte and Snowflake.

Fortune 500 Grip: 
Over 1,000 companies now spend $1M+ annually on Claude.

Efficiency: Profit margins jumped from 38% to 70% due to cheaper computing costs.

 - how to earn & benefit

Automate small businesses: 
Build custom scripts for local shops to handle their emails or inventory.

Freelance as a "Team": 
Use AI to write, code, and design, allowing you to take on 5x more clients alone.

Create Micro-Software: 
Build and sell simple, one-task apps or tools without needing a dev team.

Prompt Consulting: 
Charge non-tech companies to set up their AI workflows and "teach" the bots their business.

Content Arbitrage: 
Repurpose long videos into dozens of social posts for brands and charge for the volume.

kept.text:
OpenAI is worth $300 billion.
Your data trained it.
Your cut: $0.

This is not an oversight. The architecture was designed so that you produce the raw material, they capture the value, and you get a faster autocomplete as payment.

That deal was never negotiated. You never signed it. It was simply the only option available.

Bittensor co-founder Ala Shaabana built the one thing centralised AI companies did not want to exist:

A network where intelligence is produced on-chain, verified trustlessly, and rewarded automatically, with no company in the middle deciding who deserves what.

$TAO flows to whoever produced value.
The protocol either pays you or it does not. And it cannot lie about which one it chose.

Here is what most people have not processed yet:

-  There are 128 subnets live right now.

- Unity is building gaming assets on the network.

- DeepMind researchers are watching the decentralised training results.

- Institutions are positioning on subnet value before the public even knows what a subnet is.

- The ecosystem is now growing faster than the co-founders can track.

Read that again.

The people who built it cannot keep up with what it has become. That only happens when the incentives are so well designed that the network starts attracting people smarter than its founders.

You have seen this pattern once before. You know what happened to the people who recognised it early versus the ones who waited for permission.

$TAO is either the credible alternative to centralised AI extraction, or it is nothing.
It is nothing.

The people who understand this before it becomes obvious will not need to explain themselves later.

Who else sees this? Drop it below.

kept.text:
GPT-5.5の誕生から10日ほど経ち、GPT-5.4との出力結果の違いに悩む人も少なくないのでは。実際、GPT-5.5とGPT-5.4では、同じプロンプトでも得られる回答が異なることが公式ガイドに明記されています。以下にガイド内容をまとめましたので、GPT-5.5で効果的な回答を得るための参考にしてみてください↓

1/ 成果志向でシンプルに書く
GPT-5.5では、細かな手順説明よりも「何が良い状態なのか」「どの制約が重要か」「どのような情報が利用可能か」「最終的な回答に何を含めるべきか」といったポイントを簡潔に伝えることが鍵に。解決プロセスはモデルに委ねることで、より自然で的確な回答につながりやすくなります。

2/ 推論レベルの調整
GPT-5.5は推論効率が向上しているため、いきなり高い推論レベルに上げるのではなく、まずはlowやmediumの設定を見直すことが重要です。

3/ Responses APIのワークフロー
ツールを多用するResponses APIのワークフローでは、事前説明、phaseの扱い、assistant itemの再利用といった設計が重要です。

4/ UXのコントロール
性格設定、検索予算、検証ルールを明確にすることで、顧客向けやエージェント型のUXをより適切に設計しやすくなります。

5/ 古いプロンプト群の引き継ぎに注意
従来のプロンプトは、以前のモデルが軌道から外れないように、手順を細かく指定しすぎていることがあります。そのままGPT-5.5に引き継ぐと、それがノイズになったり、モデルの探索範囲を狭めたり、機械的な回答につながる可能性があるため注意が必要です。
https://t.co/cY4lIeSF3d

kept.text:
Andrej Karpathy: "You can outsource your thinking... but you can't outsource your understanding"

Karpathy co-founded OpenAI, got Autopilot working at Tesla, and coined the term "vibe coding." Recently he said something startling: he's never felt more behind as a programmer.

"I've been using agentic tools for a while. It was very good at chunks of code. Sometimes it would mess up and you'd have to edit them. It was kind of helpful."

Then December happened:

"I just started to notice that with the latest models... the chunks just came out fine. And then I kept asking for more and it just came out fine. And then I can't remember the last time I corrected it. And then I just trusted the system more and more. And then I was vibe coding."

On his app becoming obsolete overnight:

Karpathy built an app called Menu Gen... you take a photo of a restaurant menu, it shows you pictures of the dishes using OCR and image generation.

"I saw the software 3.0 version of this... which blew my mind. Literally just take your photo, give it to Gemini, and say 'use Nanobanana to overlay the things onto the menu.' It returned an image that is exactly the menu I took... but it rendered the pictures of the dishes directly into the pixels."

The realization:

"All of my Menu Gen is spurious. It's working in the old paradigm. That app shouldn't exist. The neural network is doing more and more of the work. Your prompt is just the image. The output is an image. There's no need to have any of the app in between."

On why AI is so jagged:

"How is it possible that state-of-the-art Opus 4.7 will simultaneously refactor a 100,000-line codebase or find zero-day vulnerabilities... and yet tells me to walk to a car wash that's 50 meters away? This is insane."

The explanation:

"We're slightly at the mercy of whatever the labs are doing... whatever they happen to put into the mix. If you're in the circuits that were part of the RL... you fly. If you're out of the data distribution... you're going to struggle."

On vibe coding vs. agentic engineering:

"Vibe coding is about raising the floor for everyone. The floor rises. Everyone can vibe code anything. That's amazing. Agentic engineering is about preserving the quality bar. You're not allowed to introduce vulnerabilities. You're still responsible for your software. But can you go faster? Spoiler... you can."

On the new ceiling:

"People used to talk about the 10x engineer. I think this is magnified a lot more. 10x is not the speedup you gain. People who are very good at this... peak a lot more than 10x."

On agents as interns:

"The agents are kind of like intern entities. You still have to be in charge of the aesthetics, the judgment, the taste."

On forgetting the details:

"I already forgot about keep_dims versus keep_dim... whether it's dim or axis... reshape or permute or transpose. I don't remember this stuff anymore. Because you don't have to. But you still have to understand what this stuff is doing at a fundamental level. The details of the APIs are handed off. You're in charge of the design. The agents are doing the fill-in-the-blanks."

On what remains valuable:

"There was a tweet that blew my mind. I keep thinking about it every other day. You can outsource your thinking... but you can't outsource your understanding."

Karpathy explains:

"I'm still part of the system. Information still has to make it into my brain. I feel like I'm becoming a bottleneck of just knowing... what are we trying to build? Why is it worth doing? How do I direct my agents? You can't be a good director if you don't understand. The LLMs don't excel at understanding. You're still uniquely in charge of that."

kept.text: Western critics only focus on instant rankings, yet overlook DeepSeek’s bigger role in fostering China’s complete AI ecosystem. Real tech strength lies in industrial layout and self-reliant supply chains, not just simple model comparisons.

profile_bio.description: Inspector on X.  Personal views.

quoted_tweet.text:
Western “AI experts” keep judging DeepSeek V4 as if the only question is whether it instantly shocked the world like last year.
So they keep asking whether V4 “beat OpenAI.”

That misses the point.

DeepSeek is no longer just a model company.
It is becoming an industrial trigger.

Around it, China is building a full AI supply chain: domestic chips, computing platforms, software frameworks, developers, enterprise adoption, and real market feedback.

That is what matters.

A model does not need to beat OpenAI overnight to change the industry.
It only needs to make Chinese companies use Chinese chips, Chinese stacks, and Chinese infrastructure at scale.

Once that happens, the feedback loop begins:
users create demand,  
demand drives deployment,  
deployment exposes problems,  
problems force iteration,  
iteration builds competence.

This is how ecosystems are born.

Nvidia understands this better than anyone.
It once fed on China’s market scale.

Now that market is being redirected toward China’s own AI stack.

profile_bio.description:

Clarity is rebellion. Memory is the weapon.

kept.text:
> TAO market cap: $3B 
> OpenAI last round: $40B 
> OpenAI IPO target: $1T

One of these is priced for what it already built. The other is priced for six months ago.

- TAO subnets are not prototypes.
They are live products - real compute, real users, real enterprise deals with Intel and PwC.

Being built quietly. While the market looks elsewhere.

- Every major tech shift followed the same pattern.
Centralised first. Then open source took over.

> Linux beat Windows Server
> Android beat everything
> Wikipedia beat Britannica

AI will not be the exception. It will be the biggest example of it.

- Closed labs have billions in capital. Open source communities have millions of developers.
History tells us which side wins.

- Bitcoin was money. Ethereum was apps. TAO is intelligence.

The pattern is the same. The opportunity is still open.
@bittensor
